repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability
repo.name: neovim

body:
Closes #37174.

Currently only has the filters `enabled` and `filetype`. If anyone has suggestions about other useful filters, let me know and I'll add them.

Also indirectly improves `:lsp enable/disable` completion by discarding invalid configs from completion.

close #37277

comment_data.body:
In this form this would put the burden of evaluating lazy/async config properties like `root_dir` to the user of `get_configs`.

In other issues it was discussed that there should probably be some kind of `get_config(name, on_config)` function. It would trigger the `on_config` callback with the fully resolved configuration. See e.g. https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/33577#issuecomment-3568292351

Imho we should go into that direction.

Evaluating lazy configs was a potential problem I considered as I was implementing this, but after testing with the entirety of nvim-lspconfig, the speed is fast enough that I cannot notice it. A warning should probably be added to this function (`Note: this will eagerly evaluate all configs`). The unfortunate truth is that there is no way to search filetypes without evaluating configs.

As an alternative, if you feel that eagerly evaluating all configs is unacceptable, I think that exposing the local `get_config_names()` added by this PR (to be used with `lsp.config[...]` and a future `lsp.get_config(...)`) would be the way to go.

Edit: Updated the documentation for `get_configs()` to warn about this behavior.
I opened #37237 as an alternative to this PR, which solves this eager loading problem while introducing some limitations. imo I still like `get_configs()`.

> Evaluating lazy configs was a potential problem I considered as I was implementing this, but after testing with the entirety of nvim-lspconfig, the speed is fast enough that I cannot notice it.

It's not the performance of loading the configs I'm worried about, but about properties that can be asynchronous. Currently that's only `root_dir` but there was talk about making the full `config` a function with callback.

Then the `get_configs` interface likely wouldn't work. We'd need a callback, unless we opt for uv lib style and made a zero-arg version that's evaluating everything synchronous. But even then I'm not sure how well that would works with some of the use-cases. E.g. afaik for rust a use-case for the `root_dir` function was to spawn cargo.

Not sure what's the best direction here. But it would be strange to end up with a `get_config` that has a callback parameter for fully evaluated configurations and a `get_configs` that returns partially evaluated configurations.

Would be interesting to see what @justinmk and @lewis6991 have to say.

I'm not sure why we need to add this at all (#37174 shows no concrete use case), and I think adding this is a mistake.

LSP configs are dynamic by design, so whenever you call this you are only ever getting a snapshot, depending on the use case this may have a significant impact.
Seems that #37277 is a better way to go then?

> Seems that #37277 is a better way to go then?

That just means consumers will use that and then eager-load all the configs anyway.

Taking a step back, I do lean towards this more general approach of modifying LSP configurations via a VSCode-friendly-approach since `.vscode/settings.json` allow for keys to be prefixed by a language identifier.

@superatomic IIUC this resolves (eager-loads) all configs, even if not needed. is_enabled intentionally *avoids* resolving configs. Is there a perf improvement possible here, by checking is_enabled without calling lsp.config?  (ofc, if filter.filetype is specified, then the config must be resolved. But otherwise...)
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -387,6 +387,76 @@ lsp.config = setmetatable({ _configs = {} }, {
   end,
 })
 
+--- @return string[]
+local function get_config_names()
+  local config_names = vim
+    .iter(api.nvim_get_runtime_file('lsp/*.lua', true))
+    --- @param path string
+    :map(function(path)
+      local file_name = path:match('[^/]*.lua$')
+      return file_name:sub(0, #file_name - 4)
+    end)
+    :totable()
+
+  vim.list_extend(config_names, vim.tbl_keys(lsp.config._configs))
+
+  return vim
+    .iter(config_names)
+    :unique()
+    --- @param name string
+    :filter(function(name)
+      return name ~= '*'
+    end)
+    :totable()
+end
+
+--- Key-value pairs used to filter the returned configs.
+--- @class vim.lsp.get_configs.Filter
+--- @inlinedoc
+---
+--- If true, only return enabled configs. If false, only return configs that
+--- aren't enabled.
+--- @field enabled? boolean
+---
+--- Only return configs which attach to the given filetype.
+--- @field filetype? string
+
+--- Get LSP configs.
+---
+--- Note: Will eagerly evaluate config files in `'runtimepath'` if necessary.
+--- @param filter? vim.lsp.get_configs.Filter
+--- @return vim.lsp.Config[]: List of |vim.lsp.Config| objects
+function lsp.get_configs(filter)
+  validate('filter', filter, 'table', true)
+
+  filter = filter or {}
+
+  local configs = {} --- @type vim.lsp.Config[]
+
+  local config_names --- @type string[]
+  if not filter.enabled then
+    config_names = get_config_names()
+  else
+    -- Shortcut filtering enabled configs by directly getting enabled configs
+    config_names = vim.tbl_keys(lsp._enabled_configs)
+  end
+
+  for _, config_name in ipairs(config_names) do
+    local config = lsp.config[config_name]

comment_data.body: The old code only checked names + `is_enabled`, thus avoided resolving configs unnecessarily.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -19,45 +19,22 @@ local function get_client_names()
     :totable()
 end
 
---- @return string[]
-local function get_config_names()
-  local config_names = vim
-    .iter(api.nvim_get_runtime_file('lsp/*.lua', true))
-    --- @param path string
-    :map(function(path)
-      local file_name = path:match('[^/]*.lua$')
-      return file_name:sub(0, #file_name - 4)
-    end)
-    :totable()
-
-  --- @diagnostic disable-next-line
-  vim.list_extend(config_names, vim.tbl_keys(lsp.config._configs))
-
-  return vim
-    .iter(config_names)
-    :unique()
-    --- @param name string
-    :filter(function(name)
-      return name ~= '*'
-    end)
-    :totable()
-end
-
---- @param filter fun(string):boolean
+--- @param filter vim.lsp.get_configs.Filter
 --- @return fun():string[]
 local function filtered_config_names(filter)
   return function()
-    return vim.iter(get_config_names()):filter(filter):totable()
+    return vim
+      .iter(lsp.get_configs(filter))
+      :map(function(config)
+        return config.name
+      end)
+      :totable()
   end
 end
 
 local complete_args = {
-  enable = filtered_config_names(function(name)
-    return not lsp.is_enabled(name)
-  end),
-  disable = filtered_config_names(function(name)
-    return lsp.is_enabled(name)
-  end),
+  enable = filtered_config_names { enabled = false },
+  disable = filtered_config_names { enabled = true },

comment_data.body: If a config is enabled it is already loaded, and `get_configs()` will use the internal list of enabled configs instead of the list of all configs if `enabled = true`. So when filtering by enabled configs, nothing is eagerly loaded.

comment_data.body:
I'm hoping we can change this to:


```suggestion
--- Note: Will eagerly evaluate config files in `'runtimepath'` if `filetype` filter is specified.
```

comment_data.body: This was an intentional change that sacrificed performance for accuracy (skipping invalid configs), as mentioned in the PR. In practice I found that when using nvim-lspconfig, this was fast enough to not be noticeable. If you think completion shouldn't evaluate the config, I can open a PR to restore the original behavior.

comment_data.body: Not possible, since it returns a list of resolved configs. That was why I was proposing `vim.lsp.get_config_names()` (#37277), which would have had this property.

comment_data.body: Sorry I was a bit unclear. Based on https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/37237#discussion_r2975315735 , I was worried that `get_configs(filter.enabled=true)` resolved ALL configs (including disabled). But it looks like it only resolves enabled configs, which is good.

comment_data.body: Correct. I specifically included that optimization. And technically it doesn't even resolve them, because `vim.lsp.enable()` would have had to resolve the config beforehand to be able to enable them.

comment_regular_data.body:
> But it would be strange to end up with a `get_config` that has a callback parameter for fully evaluated configurations and a `get_configs` that returns partially evaluated configurations.

That's true. And that's a hint that we should focus on `get_config` instead of rushing this other thing.

I think the discussion of `get_config` is mostly settled and waiting on implementation. It could take an optional filter and return multiple configs.

I'll update the docs to mention the "partial state" caveat. To reiterate, my understanding (correct if wrong) is : 

- because of technical reality, returning "possibly (likely) incomplete" configs is the best we can do and there is no path to a better approach. 
    - declining to add `get_configs` just means that users have to implement their own approach with the same limitation.
- we can add an `on_config` parameter later, that isn't blocked.

> because of technical reality, returning "possibly (likely) incomplete" configs is the best we can do and there is no path to a better approach.

As I understand it, the configs are accurate as of the time `get_configs()` is called, as the values that are returned are the same as the one given by `vim.lsp.config[...]` (which I'm pretty sure should be accurate).

Perhaps I'm missing something? To be honest, I'm not entirely sure what [the problem that @mfussenegger mentions](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/37237#discussion_r2668544636) is.

> the configs are accurate as of the time `get_configs()` is called

One problem is if the user calls get_configs() too "early" in their init, before later `vim.lsp.config()` calls are done, they will get an incomplete picture.

That is true, yes. imo it's expected for `get_configs()` to behave like this (it returns a simple table and cannot read the future), but mentioning it in passing in the docs can't hurt.

I'm just not sure what this means:

> [• Configs may be in a partial state if they have async properties such as `on_dir()`.](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/38370/changes#diff-6b5f3071d65558aab177912061ac6a2f5312660655a449276c83697686f28e72R1161-R1162)

If `on_dir()` is referring to the argument of `root_dir` when it is a function, I don't see how this is applicable. The `root_dir` function does not need to be called for `get_configs()` to work.

repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability
repo.name: neovim

title: feat(lsp): add `vim.lsp.get_configs()`
